data.title: IonQ: The Most Advanced Quantum Computing Stock, With Upside Even After Multiple Compression
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe IonQ (IONQ) is the most advanced quantum computing company today, leveraging trapped ion technology with likely the lowest error rates and real cloud-based commercialization.

While its valuation remains elevated and technical risks persist, even after assuming ratio compression and conservative growth, my fair value for the stock is $65 per share, offering about 35% upside.

Background

IonQ is a quantum computing company that bases its quantum technology on trapped ions, where individual ions are manipulated with lasers to serve as qubits. It’s the most advanced quantum stock, already providing quantum systems accessible through the cloud via Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud. IonQ is focused on building and operating quantum processors and monetizing them as a service.

Right now, trapped ions technology is the most advanced quantum technology because it has the lowest error rates today and already has applications in research; however, it has some limitations, and that is why I believe neutral atom (Infleqtion (CCCX) technology, about which I have written a couple of articles), will be better in the future.

The main trapped ions limitations are related to scalability, which is difficult. Moreover, the hardware is difficult to miniaturize and manufacture on a large scale due to its reliance on precision lasers and complex optics.

On the other hand, neutral atoms are easier to scale up as the machines required are simpler and cheaper, and their main weakness right now is that their accuracy is lower.

However, experts suggest that different technologies will coexist, and I believe trapped ions and neutral atoms are the best positioned. I recommend reading this article that explains the main technologies.

The company expects to reach 256 physical qubits this year, more than doubling its current levels, and it has an even more aggressive roadmap for the future. IonQ expects to scale to 10,000 physical qubits by 2027 and then interconnect chips to reach more than 2 million physical qubits by 2030.

If the company achieves those targets, which I consider quite ambitious, revenue should continue to grow at attractive rates.

Moreover, the company is very active in the M&A market and in 2025 has closed six acquisitions that allow IonQ to further grow revenues and create a full quantum technology ecosystem, not only focused on quantum computing but expanding to quantum sensing and secure communications.

And it seems that 2026 will follow the same path, as the company has already announced its first deal with the acquisition of SkyWater (SKYT). I consider this deal a steal and highly accretive for IonQ, which is buying the only US-based pure-play technology foundry specialized in quantum computing. Moreover, the company recently announced a collaboration on the development of a 3D chip, which could further increase revenues in the future. I believe that this acquisition is the best one IonQ has done so far and could justify a large percentage of IonQ's future valuation.

Financial Position and Valuation

IonQ has a strong financial position with $3.5 billion in cash and no debt, after raising $2 billion in October. What is astonishing is that the company managed to issue the shares at a 20% premium to the previous close price and above its all-time high.

After such a successful raise and with the huge cash pile, I don’t expect the company to issue more shares in secondary offerings unless it acquires another big company.

Regarding valuation, like most quantum stocks, the company trades at astonishing valuations. However, despite being the most consolidated quantum computing company, it trades at the second cheapest valuation after Infleqtion.

Moreover, the company expects to reach above $100 million in revenues in 2025, which will further reduce its P/S ratio to near 150x.

Assuming the company continues to improve its products and achieves its 256 qubit system, I expect IonQ to reach at least $150 million in 2026. I believe this is a conservative number, as I expect the company to use its huge cash pile for acquisitions, increasing its revenues.

But even with this conservative assumption and even compressing the P/S ratio to 150x, my fair value is $65 per share, representing around a 35% upside.

Risks

The main risk is the elevated valuation, which already discounts a very optimistic long-term outcome. Any disappointment in technical milestones, revenue growth, or overall sector sentiment could lead to sharp multiple compression.

Another risk is that quantum computing is still a new technology and there is no guarantee that trapped‑ion systems will scale as expected or will be the future winner.

Finally, IonQ is still burning cash, and this will probably be the case for many years. This makes dilution inevitable and causes the company to depend on market conditions. If those change at a time when the company needs cash, the dilution could become significant.

This is why I have allocated only a small percentage of the portfolio to IonQ, as risks are considerable.

Conclusion

IonQ is the most promising quantum computing company in the short term. It is already the quantum company with the highest revenues, and it has a clear roadmap to reach 2 million physical qubits by the end of the decade.

While the valuation is demanding, it is lower than some of its peers with worse prospects. Even assuming a valuation compression and limited growth in 2026, the company could provide positive returns. My fair value, based on conservative assumptions, is $65 per share, representing around a 35% upside.

data.title: REZ: ETF Following Housing Trends
data.description:
Fast Facts

iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ) was launched on 5/1/2007 and tracks the FTSE Nareit All Residential Capped Index. REZ has a portfolio of 39 real estate investment trusts (REITs), a 30-day SEC yield of 2.53%, and an expense ratio of 0.48%. It is a mid-size and quite liquid ETF, with $785 million of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $5.8 million. The fund’s issuer, iShares, is a part of BlackRock, Inc. (BLK), with over 1500 ETFs and $4.2 trillion of AUM globally.

Underlying Index

As described by FTSE Russell, eligible securities must be tax-qualified real REITs listed on the New York Stock Exchange or NASDAQ, have at least $150 million in market capitalization, meet liquidity and float requirements, and be classified in one of the following real estate sectors: residential apartments, manufactured homes, healthcare, and self-storage.

The index is weighted based on free-float market capitalization and rebalanced quarterly with limits of 22.5% in any constituent and 45% in those exceeding 5%. The fund’s turnover rate was 13% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark iShares US Real Estate ETF (IYR).

Portfolio

The portfolio has a tilt to mid-cap companies (47% of asset value), with lower exposure in large caps (21%) and small/micro caps (32%). The fund has about 45% of its assets in healthcare REITs (hospitals, medical facilities, senior and disabled housing), 37% in rental apartments and homes, and 18% in self-storage.

The portfolio is very concentrated and has significant company risk, especially in the largest issuer. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 67.4% of asset value, with Welltower Inc. (WELL) weighting 20.26%.

The portfolio composition is given as an example from 1/23/2026. Weights may have changed by the time you read this.

Fundamentals

Neither iShares nor major data providers report specific real estate metrics (P/FFO, P/AFFO) for ETFs, so we have to rely on traditional valuation and growth ratios. P/FFO and P/AFFO reflect these holdings' actual cash generation quality, so this piece of missing information on the ETF valuation level creates some challenges to seeing the full, accurate picture. REZ is close to IYR in price-to-book and price-to-cash flow and superior in cash flow growth.

Distributions have gone sideways over the last decade, except for a better year in 2016. The 10-year dividend growth rate of 12.4% has lagged inflation (about 38%, based on CPI).

However, REZ did much better than IYR, whose annualized distribution decreased by 24% in the same time.

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of REZ and two thematic housing ETFs:

  • The Hoya Capital Housing ETF (HOMZ)
  • Residential REIT ETF (HAUS)

HAUS is exclusively invested in equity REITs, just like REZ, and is actively managed. HOMZ has only about 35% of assets in the real estate sector and 65% in housing-related companies classified in various sectors (mostly consumer discretionary, financials, and industrials).
